agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of a delivery platform's core domain model from a 1:1 Shipment-to-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental structural change to the Shipment aggregate root, affecting data models, APIs, partner integrations, and 2 million existing production records. Multi-recipient shipments are common in logistics (e.g., split deliveries, gift shipments to multiple addresses, B2B distribution runs), but the transition carries significant risk.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A logistics unit representing goods being transported from origin to one or more destinations. The aggregate root that owns Recipients."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A party who receives a delivery. Identified by name, address, and phone number. Now may also carry delivery preferences."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-specific instructions governing how, when, or where a delivery should occur (e.g., time windows, signature requirements, safe-place instructions). A NEW domain concept introduced by this change."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"UNDEFINED — the model evolution does not clarify whether one recipient is primary/default when a shipment has multiple recipients. This is a critical ambiguity."},{"term":"Multi-Recipient Shipment","definition":"A shipment with more than one recipient, implying multiple delivery stops or split deliveries."},{"term":"Partner API","definition":"External integration point that currently consumes shipment data in the single-recipient format."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A shipment with zero recipients has no delivery destination and is physically undeliverable — the aggregate is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT shipment.recipients.count >= 1 for every Shipment in any state (draft, in-transit, delivered)."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment must have a valid name, address, and phone number.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These are the minimum fields required to execute a physical delivery. Without them, a courier cannot complete the delivery.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT every Recipient entity has non-empty name, non-empty address, and valid phone number."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment may independently specify delivery preferences.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Delivery preferences are a business policy enhancement; a delivery can technically succeed without explicit preferences by falling back to defaults.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that when Recipient A has preference 'leave at door' and Recipient B has preference 'require signature', each preference is independently stored and retrievable per recipient."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Existing single-recipient shipments must remain valid and queryable after the model change with no data loss or behavioral change.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"2 million production records represent real business obligations. Corrupting or invalidating them breaks delivery contracts.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that all 2M existing shipments are queryable post-migration and return the same recipient data as before."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Partner APIs must continue to receive shipment data they can parse without breaking their integrations.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Breaking partner integrations violates contractual obligations and causes real-world delivery failures across the supply chain.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that partner API payloads for single-recipient shipments are identical to the pre-change format, OR partners have been migrated to a new contract version."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The Recipient collection on a Shipment must not contain duplicate recipients (same name + same address).","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Business policy to prevent accidental double-delivery, but edge cases may exist (e.g., two packages to same address for different people with same name).","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that adding a Recipient with identical name and address to an existing Recipient on the same Shipment triggers a warning or rejection."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"the user adds one or more Recipients with delivery preferences","then":"the Shipment is persisted with all Recipients and their individual preferences intact"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An existing single-recipient Shipment from before the migration","when":"it is retrieved via any read path (UI, API, report)","then":"it returns the original Recipient data unchanged, as if it were a multi-recipient shipment with exactly one recipient"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Shipment with multiple Recipients","when":"the shipment data is sent to an external partner API","then":"the data is formatted according to the partner's supported contract version (legacy single-recipient format if partner has not upgraded, or new multi-recipient format if they have)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Shipment with multiple Recipients","when":"a user attempts to remove the last remaining Recipient","then":"the operation is rejected and the Shipment retains its Recipient (minimum-one invariant enforced)"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Shipment is in-transit","when":"a new Recipient is added or an existing Recipient's preferences are modified","then":"the system enforces business rules about whether mid-transit modifications are allowed and notifies affected couriers"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment","when":"one Recipient's delivery succeeds and another fails","then":"the Shipment tracks delivery status independently per Recipient, and the overall Shipment status reflects the composite state accurately"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The data migration of 2M existing shipments","when":"the migration completes","then":"every existing shipment has exactly one Recipient in the new collection structure, with delivery preferences set to system defaults, and zero data loss"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment has 3 Recipients but the courier can only deliver to 2 due to route constraints","expected_behavior":"The system must handle partial delivery states — some Recipients delivered, others pending or failed — without corrupting the Shipment's overall status."},{"scenario":"Two Recipients on the same Shipment have the same physical address but different delivery preferences","expected_behavior":"Both Recipients are valid. Delivery preferences are honored per-Recipient, not per-address. The courier receives distinct instructions for each."},{"scenario":"A partner API receives a multi-recipient shipment but only supports single-recipient format","expected_behavior":"The system must either send multiple single-recipient payloads (one per recipient) or send only to partners that support the new format. The mapping strategy must be defined."},{"scenario":"Recipient is removed from a multi-recipient shipment after goods have already been dispatched for that recipient","expected_behavior":"Domain must define whether removal is a soft-delete (cancelled delivery) or hard-delete, and what happens to in-transit goods. Likely requires a 'cancelled' delivery status per recipient."},{"scenario":"Migration runs on 2M records and fails partway through","expected_behavior":"Migration must be idempotent and resumable. Partially migrated data must not leave shipments in an inconsistent state (some with old schema, some with new)."},{"scenario":"A Shipment with 50+ Recipients (bulk distribution)","expected_behavior":"System must define upper bounds. Without a max-recipient limit, performance and UX degrade. Business should define a reasonable ceiling."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High impact. Multi-recipient shipments may enable new revenue streams (group deliveries, B2B distribution), but the migration and API versioning carry significant engineering cost. Failed migration or broken partner integrations could cause delivery failures translating to SLA penalties and customer refunds.","operational":"Major operational change. Couriers now need routing logic for multi-stop deliveries. Dispatch, tracking, and reporting systems all need to handle N recipients per shipment. Status tracking becomes per-recipient rather than per-shipment, increasing operational complexity.","user":"Customers gain flexibility (ship to multiple people in one order). However, UI must clearly communicate per-recipient status. Couriers need updated mobile apps showing multi-stop routes. Partner portal users need updated documentation and potentially new integration work.","risk":"HIGH. This is a breaking change to the core aggregate root. Risks include: (1) data migration failure on 2M records, (2) partner API breakage causing supply chain disruption, (3) incomplete status model leading to 'phantom deliveries' where shipment shows complete but one recipient never received goods, (4) performance degradation from N+1 query patterns on the new collection relationship.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers","impact":"Gain multi-recipient capability but may experience disruption if migration causes data issues with existing shipments."},{"group":"Courier / Drivers","impact":"Must handle multi-stop deliveries. Their apps, routing, and proof-of-delivery workflows all change. Training required."},{"group":"External Partners","impact":"API contract changes. Partners must either be shielded by backward-compatible formatting or given migration timelines for new API versions. Breaking partners = delivery failures."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch Team","impact":"New complexity in shipment assignment, route optimization, and status monitoring. Dashboards and alerting must account for per-recipient status."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Pricing model may need revision. Is a multi-recipient shipment one charge or N charges? Invoicing and revenue recognition affected."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Schema migration, API versioning, backward compatibility layer, new status state machine, updated tests across all services."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The product team's requirement is clearly stated at a high level ('one or more Recipients, each with delivery preferences'), but critical domain details are unspecified. The concept of 'delivery preferences' is undefined, the interaction between multi-recipient status and overall shipment status is unspecified, and the partner API migration strategy is not addressed. The 2M record migration adds risk. Confidence is low because the spec introduces a fundamental model change without addressing the second-order effects.","high_confidence_areas":["The minimum-one-recipient invariant is clear and non-negotiable","Backward compatibility for existing data is a hard requirement","Partner API disruption risk is real and must be mitigated","Per-recipient delivery preferences is a valid domain concept"],"low_confidence_areas":["What exactly constitutes 'delivery preferences' — time windows? signature requirements? safe-place? All of the above?","Whether there should be a 'primary recipient' concept for backward compatibility and default behavior","How shipment-level status is derived from per-recipient statuses (all-delivered? any-delivered? majority?)","Upper bound on recipients per shipment","Billing model for multi-recipient shipments","Whether mid-transit recipient modifications are allowed"],"assumptions":["Delivery preferences will include at minimum: time window, signature requirement, and special instructions","The migration will be online (no downtime) given 2M records","Partners will be given a deprecation timeline rather than an immediate breaking change","Existing single-recipient shipments will be treated as multi-recipient with a collection of size 1 post-migration"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery preferences' is introduced as a new domain concept but never defined. It could mean time-window preferences, signature requirements, safe-place instructions, contactless delivery, or any combination. The scope of this concept fundamentally shapes the Recipient entity and every downstream system.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Couriers","End Customers","Product Team"],"options":["Define a fixed set of delivery preference fields (time window, signature required, special instructions)","Define delivery preferences as a flexible key-value structure allowing future extensibility","Start with a minimal set and expand iteratively"],"recommendation":"Define a concrete, bounded set of delivery preferences before implementation begins. Recommend starting with: preferred time window, signature requirement (boolean), and free-text special instructions. Extensibility can be added later."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The relationship between per-recipient delivery status and overall shipment status is undefined. When Recipient A's delivery succeeds but Recipient B's fails, what is the Shipment's status? This is critical for tracking, reporting, SLAs, and billing.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","End Customers","Finance","Engineering"],"options":["Shipment status = worst-case (any failed = shipment failed)","Shipment status = best-case (any delivered = shipment partially delivered)","Introduce a new 'partially delivered' shipment status","Shipment has no aggregate status; only per-recipient statuses exist"],"recommendation":"Introduce a 'partially_delivered' status at the Shipment level. Shipment is 'delivered' only when ALL recipients are delivered. This preserves SLA clarity and prevents premature closure."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partner API backward compatibility strategy is unspecified. External partners currently consume single-recipient payloads. The spec does not address whether to version the API, provide a compatibility shim, or require partner migration.","affected_stakeholders":["External Partners","Engineering","Operations"],"options":["API versioning: v1 returns single-recipient (first/primary), v2 returns full collection","Compatibility shim: always send array but partners on old contract receive only first element","Forced migration: all partners upgrade within a deadline"],"recommendation":"Implement API versioning. Maintain v1 for existing partners with a 6-12 month deprecation window. New partners onboard to v2 immediately. v1 returns the first recipient to preserve backward compatibility."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.text: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of a delivery platform's core domain model from a 1:1 Shipment-to-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental structural change to the Shipment aggregate root, affecting data models, APIs, partner integrations, and 2 million existing production records. Multi-recipient shipments are common in logistics (e.g., split deliveries, gift shipments to multiple addresses, B2B distribution runs), but the transition carries significant risk.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A logistics unit representing goods being transported from origin to one or more destinations. Now may also carry delivery preferences."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-specific instructions governing how, when, or where a delivery should occur (e.g., time windows, signature requirements, safe-place instructions). A NEW domain concept introduced by this change."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"UNDEFINED — the model evolution does not clarify whether one recipient is primary/default when a shipment has multiple recipients. This is a critical ambiguity."},{"term":"Multi-Recipient Shipment","definition":"A shipment with more than one recipient, implying multiple delivery stops or split deliveries."},{"term":"Partner API","definition":"External integration point that currently consumes shipment data in the single-recipient format."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A shipment with zero recipients has no delivery destination and is physically undeliverable — the aggregate is in a corrupt state.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT shipment.recipients.count >= 1 for every Shipment in any state (draft, in-transit, delivered)."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient on a Shipment must have a valid name, address, and phone number.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These are the minimum fields required to execute a physical delivery. Corrupting or invalidating them breaks delivery contracts.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that all 2M existing shipments are queryable post-migration and return the same recipient data as before."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Partner APIs must continue to receive shipment data they can parse without breaking their integrations.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Breaking partner integrations violates contractual obligations and causes real-world delivery failures across the supply chain.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that partner API payloads for single-recipient shipments are identical to the pre-change format, OR partners have been migrated to a new contract version."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The Recipient collection on a Shipment must not contain duplicate recipients (same name + same address).","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Business policy to prevent accidental double-delivery, but edge cases may exist (e.g., two packages to same address for different people with same name).","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that adding a Recipient with identical name and address to an existing Recipient on the same Shipment triggers a warning or rejection."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"the user adds one or more Recipients with delivery preferences","then":"the Shipment is persisted with all Recipients and their individual preferences intact"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An existing single-recipient Shipment from before the migration","when":"it is retrieved via any read path (UI, API, report)","then":"it returns the original Recipient data unchanged, as if it were a multi-recipient shipment with exactly one recipient"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Shipment with multiple Recipients","when":"the shipment data is sent to an external partner API","then":"the data is formatted according to the partner's supported contract version (legacy single-recipient format if partner has not upgraded, or new multi-recipient format if they have)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Shipment with multiple Recipients","when":"a user attempts to remove the last remaining Recipient","then":"the operation is rejected and the Shipment retains its Recipient (minimum-one invariant enforced)"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Shipment is in-transit","when":"a new Recipient is added or an existing Recipient's preferences are modified","then":"the system enforces business rules about whether mid-transit modifications are allowed and notifies affected couriers"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment","when":"one Recipient's delivery succeeds and another fails","then":"the Shipment tracks delivery status independently per Recipient, and the overall Shipment status reflects the composite state accurately"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"The data migration of 2M existing shipments","when":"the migration completes","then":"every existing shipment has exactly one Recipient in the new collection structure, with delivery preferences set to system defaults, and zero data loss"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A Shipment has 3 Recipients but the courier can only deliver to 2 due to route constraints","expected_behavior":"The system must handle partial delivery states — some Recipients delivered, others pending or failed — without corrupting the Shipment's overall status."},{"scenario":"Two Recipients on the same Shipment have the same physical address but different delivery preferences","expected_behavior":"Both Recipients are valid. Delivery preferences are honored per-Recipient, not per-address. The courier receives distinct instructions for each."},{"scenario":"A partner API receives a multi-recipient shipment but only supports single-recipient format","expected_behavior":"The system must either send multiple single-recipient payloads (one per recipient) or send only to partners that support the new format. Partially migrated data must not leave shipments in an inconsistent state (some with old schema, some with new)."},{"scenario":"A Shipment with 50+ Recipients (bulk distribution)","expected_behavior":"System must define upper bounds. Without a max-recipient limit, performance and UX degrade. Business should define a reasonable ceiling."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High impact. Multi-recipient shipments may enable new revenue streams (group deliveries, B2B distribution), but the migration and API versioning carry significant engineering cost. Failed migration or broken partner integrations could cause delivery failures translating to SLA penalties and customer refunds.","operational":"Major operational change. Couriers now need routing logic for multi-stop deliveries. Dispatch, tracking, and reporting systems all need to handle N recipients per shipment. Status tracking becomes per-recipient rather than per-shipment, increasing operational complexity.","user":"Customers gain flexibility (ship to multiple people in one order). However, UI must clearly communicate per-recipient status. Couriers need updated mobile apps showing multi-stop routes. Partner portal users need updated documentation and potentially new integration work.","risk":"HIGH. This is a breaking change to the core aggregate root. Risks include: (1) data migration failure on 2M records, (2) partner API breakage causing supply chain disruption, (3) incomplete status model leading to 'phantom deliveries' where shipment shows complete but one recipient never received goods, (4) performance degradation from N+1 query patterns on the new collection relationship.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers","impact":"Gain multi-recipient capability but may experience disruption if migration causes data issues with existing shipments."},{"group":"Courier / Drivers","impact":"Must handle multi-stop deliveries. Their apps, routing, and proof-of-delivery workflows all change. Training required."},{"group":"External Partners","impact":"API contract changes. Partners must either be shielded by backward-compatible formatting or given migration timelines for new API versions. Breaking partners = delivery failures."},{"group":"Operations / Dispatch Team","impact":"New complexity in shipment assignment, route optimization, and status monitoring. Dashboards and alerting must account for per-recipient status."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Pricing model may need revision. Is a multi-recipient shipment one charge or N charges? Invoicing and revenue recognition affected."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Schema migration, API versioning, backward compatibility layer, new status state machine, updated tests across all services."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The product team's requirement is clearly stated at a high level ('one or more Recipients, each with delivery preferences'), but critical domain details are unspecified. The concept of 'delivery preferences' is undefined, the interaction between multi-recipient status and overall shipment status is unspecified, and the partner API migration strategy is not addressed. The 2M record migration adds risk. Confidence is low because the spec introduces a fundamental model change without addressing the second-order effects.","high_confidence_areas":["The minimum-one-recipient invariant is clear and non-negotiable","Backward compatibility for existing data is a hard requirement","Partner API disruption risk is real and must be mitigated","Per-recipient delivery preferences is a valid domain concept"],"low_confidence_areas":["What exactly constitutes 'delivery preferences' — time windows? signature requirements? safe-place? All of the above?","Whether there should be a 'primary recipient' concept for backward compatibility and default behavior","How shipment-level status is derived from per-recipient statuses (all-delivered? any-delivered? majority?)","Upper bound on recipients per shipment","Billing model for multi-recipient shipments","Whether mid-transit recipient modifications are allowed"],"assumptions":["Delivery preferences will include at minimum: time window, signature requirement, and special instructions","The migration will be online (no downtime) given 2M records","Partners will be given a deprecation timeline rather than an immediate breaking change","Existing single-recipient shipments will be treated as multi-recipient with a collection of size 1 post-migration"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery preferences' is introduced as a new domain concept but never defined. It could mean time-window preferences, signature requirements, safe-place instructions, contactless delivery, or any combination. The scope of this concept fundamentally shapes the Recipient entity and every downstream system.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Couriers","End Customers","Product Team"],"options":["Define a fixed set of delivery preference fields (time window, signature required, special instructions)","Define delivery preferences as a flexible key-value structure allowing future extensibility","Start with a minimal set and expand iteratively"],"recommendation":"Define a concrete, bounded set of delivery preferences before implementation begins. Recommend starting with: preferred time window, signature requirement (boolean), and free-text special instructions. Extensibility can be added later."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The relationship between per-recipient delivery status and overall shipment status is undefined. When Recipient A's delivery succeeds but Recipient B's fails, what is the Shipment's status? This is critical for tracking, reporting, SLAs, and billing.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","End Customers","Finance","Engineering"],"options":["Shipment status = worst-case (any failed = shipment failed)","Shipment status = best-case (any delivered = shipment partially delivered)","Introduce a new 'partially delivered' shipment status","Shipment has no aggregate status; only per-recipient statuses exist"],"recommendation":"Introduce a 'partially_delivered' status at the Shipment level. Shipment is 'delivered' only when ALL recipients are delivered. This preserves SLA clarity and prevents premature closure."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partner API backward compatibility strategy is unspecified. External partners currently consume single-recipient payloads. The spec does not address whether to version the API, provide a compatibility shim, or require partner migration.","affected_stakeholders":["External Partners","Engineering","Operations"],"options":["API versioning: v1 returns single-recipient (first/primary), v2 returns full collection","Compatibility shim: always send array but partners on old contract receive only first element","Forced migration: all partners upgrade within a deadline"],"recommendation":"Implement API versioning. Maintain v1 for existing partners with a 6-12 month deprecation window. New partners onboard to v2 immediately. v1 returns the first recipient to preserve backward compatibility."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
